summary:
Bacteriocidal toxins from pathogens such as Bacillus cereus predominantly impact regions directly exposed to their release; thus they are generally present in zones of infection or contamination after initial exposure through ingestion or contact. Toxins tend not to localize strictly but spread according to pathogen activity and host response patterns across various potential sites including gut mucosa for foodborne illness cases. Key point here relates specifically to broad distribution based upon mode of entry versus fixed localization at one site alone due to systemic nature often seen clinically during infectious processes involving exotoxin production leading to multi-organ involvement potentially depending also on dose received initially via route taken up e.g., oral vs dermal etc.. In summary, while exact location varies per case scenario, general principle holds true regarding widespread presence correlating closely linked back towards source material ingested/infected tissue
